title: Anthropic shutdown reshapes the AI access debate, G7 pivots on minerals and AI trust
subhead: The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two most capable models over a 'fix this code' jailbreak, sending allies scrambling for access guarantees and boosting Chinese rivals. The G7 set its first critical minerals dependency targets and Japan pushed a joint stockpiling plan, while the UK banned social media for under-16s and social media overtook traditional news globally.

sections.heading: Anthropic model shutdown: the 'fix this code' export control that stunned AI
sections.body:
On June 12, the US Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to suspend all access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national, anywhere in the world — including noncitizen Anthropic employees inside the US. Because the restriction could not be enforced selectively on a shared cloud service, Anthropic pulled both models for all customers globally, according to its official statement.

The trigger: a jailbreak discovered by Amazon researchers and reported to the White House. According to cybersecurity expert Katie Moussouris, who reviewed the finding at Anthropic's request, the entire technique amounted to three words — 'fix this code' — which prompted Fable to patch software vulnerabilities it had initially refused to analyze. Moussouris argued this capability was 'the most valuable thing an AI model can do for defensive security' and could not meaningfully be removed without weakening the model for defenders (Fortune).

White House AI adviser David Sacks claimed Anthropic had refused to fix the issue; Anthropic disputed this, saying it was never given specific details and never refused. The company argued that the same narrow capability was available from other publicly deployed models, including OpenAI's GPT-5.5, without any jailbreak (Forbes, Anthropic statement). About 100 cybersecurity professionals from Nvidia, Adobe, Google, and other companies signed an open letter calling for the export controls to be rescinded (Fortune).

The episode sits in a broader context: Anthropic had previously been designated a 'supply chain risk' by the Pentagon after refusing to allow its models to be used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. A federal judge temporarily blocked that designation in March, calling it 'Orwellian' (Reason, WIRED). The export control order is the most consequential government intervention yet in a commercially deployed AI model.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the US has used export control powers against a specific AI model rather than chips. The global shutdown mechanism — where restricting foreign access effectively kills the product for everyone — sets a precedent that could apply to any frontier model with foreign users.

sections.heading: G7 allies scramble for AI access as US tightens the gate
sections.body:
On the sidelines of the G7 summit opening dinner in Evian-les-Bains on June 16, representatives of several member countries raised with US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick the idea of a 'trusted partners' scheme that would let allied nations or companies access the most advanced American AI models, according to people familiar with the discussions (TNW, Reuters).

The proposal is a direct reaction to the Anthropic shutdown. Because the Commerce Department directive covered foreign nationals of all countries — including close US allies — the G7 governments discovered that access to the frontier models they rely on for cybersecurity and defense can be cut off without consultation. As TNW put it: 'America's closest allies have discovered that the most advanced AI they can buy is American, that access to it is now a lever in Washington's hand, and that the only available response is to ask, politely, for it back.'

Nothing has been agreed. The discussions were exploratory, and a trusted-partners arrangement remains an idea floated over dinner. But the framing is notable: broader access to frontier models would let G7 countries build stronger cyber-defenses against rivals, pitched in Washington's own security language (TNW).
sections.whyItMatters: AI model access is becoming a geopolitical lever comparable to chip export controls. The difference: chip restrictions target hardware that takes years to replicate; model access can be revoked overnight. Allies now want contractual guarantees before they build critical infrastructure on American AI.

sections.heading: Zhipu surges 33% as China positions itself as the open-AI alternative
sections.body:
Chinese AI lab Zhipu — listed in Hong Kong as Knowledge Atlas Technology — saw its shares surge as much as 48% before closing up 33% on June 15, as Wall Street banks bet that Washington's clampdown on Anthropic would redirect demand toward Chinese models (Bloomberg, TNW, CNBC).

The timing was sharp: days after Anthropic pulled Fable and Mythos, Zhipu announced it would release GLM-5.2, its most capable model, as open-source software with no usage restrictions. 'Cutting-edge intelligence should not belong to only a few, nor should it be withdrawn at any time,' the company said (TNW).

JPMorgan raised its price target on Zhipu to HK$1,400 from HK$950 while downgrading domestic rival MiniMax. Bank of America initiated coverage on both with buy ratings. The shared thesis: as US frontier models become harder to access, China captures the value-for-money segment. Zhipu's stock has risen more than tenfold since its January IPO, reaching a market value around HK$489 billion (CNBC, TNW).

Z-Ben Advisors estimates around 40% of US-based AI engineers were born in China, and the new directive effectively bars many of them from the systems they helped build — a recipe, the firm warns, for 'brain flight' toward Chinese labs like DeepSeek and Moonshot (CNBC).
sections.whyItMatters: Every door the US closes on model access, China is rushing to hold open. The open-versus-closed split in AI is becoming a geopolitical dividing line as clear as the one that once defined internet architecture.

sections.heading: G7 sets first critical minerals dependency targets, Japan pushes stockpiling
sections.body:
For the first time, G7 leaders endorsed a measurable supply chain resilience target: reducing dependence on any single non-G7 supplier of rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030, with a longer-term objective of 50%. For all other critical minerals, relevant ministers were tasked with establishing dependency-reduction targets before year-end (CSIS, G7 Leaders Declaration).

The target is ambitious. China currently controls 93% of permanent magnet production globally and an estimated 85-91% of rare earth refining (CSIS, IEA). G7 nations account for over half of global rare earth magnet imports (Fortune).

Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi proposed a Joint Stockpiling Cooperation Initiative for critical minerals including rare earths, aimed at countering China's export controls. Japan has been on the receiving end of those controls: Chinese rare earth exports to Japan fell 88% in March and 82% in April year-on-year, according to Nikkei Asia (Global Times, Sankei Shimbun).

CSIS notes that building refining capacity outside China is the real bottleneck. Japan has spent over 15 years trying to de-risk its supply chains, but only recently started shifting reliance to Australia, South Korea, and Vietnam. Even experienced producers like Lynas Rare Earths have shelved a Pentagon-funded heavy rare earth facility in Texas after permitting troubles and cost overruns (CSIS).

The G7 did not agree on a coordinated price floor mechanism. Governments were reluctant to use public funds, and passing costs to downstream industries like automotive risked undermining competitiveness (CSIS).
sections.whyItMatters: Setting a 60% threshold is the first time the G7 has put a number on minerals decoupling. The challenge is not mining — it is refining, where China's dominance exceeds 90%. Japan's 15-year diversification effort shows how slow this transition is even with sustained political commitment.

sections.heading: UK bans social media for under-16s as digital regulation escalates
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a sweeping ban on social media for children under 16, covering Facebook, Instagram, X, TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube. The ban — expected to take effect in spring 2027 — also prohibits livestreaming and stranger contact features for under-16s, and raises the minimum age for chatbots that imitate romantic interactions to 18. WhatsApp and Signal are exempted (WIRED, UK Government).

The move follows Australia's similar ban imposed last November, but early evidence from Down Under is cautionary: an eSafety Commission study found that 70% of under-16s in Australia continued to use social media despite the ban (WIRED).

Over 90% of parents who responded to the government's public consultation supported an outright ban. But critics call it a blunt instrument. The Molly Rose Foundation argued the ban does not address addictive platform design; the University of Surrey's Emily Setty warned it could be 'performative' (WIRED). YouTube and Snapchat both pushed back, arguing that bans push children toward less safe, unregulated platforms.

The government announced the measures before releasing its full consultation findings, which it promised to publish by end of summer. A former Starmer adviser told WIRED the PM rushed the ban to shore up parliamentary support amid leadership challenges.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK becomes the second major democracy to legislate a social media age ban. If the Australian precedent is any guide, enforcement will be the real test — and the gap between legislative intent and actual behavior could be wide.

sections.heading: Social media overtakes traditional news sources worldwide
sections.body:
The 2026 Digital News Report from Oxford's Reuters Institute, based on surveys of nearly 100,000 people across 48 countries, marks what lead author Jim Egan calls 'a significant milestone': 54% of respondents said they got news from social media or video platforms in the past week, overtaking TV news at 52%, news websites and apps at 51%, and radio at 21%. Including AI chatbots pushes the digital figure to 56% (Reuters Institute, Digital Journal, Nieman Lab).

The shift is generational. Among 18-24 year-olds, half say social media is their main news source; among those over 55, TV still leads. None of the age brackets said publisher sites and apps were their first port of call (Digital Journal).

Chatbot use for news is rising but still marginal as a primary source: 10% of respondents said they used AI chatbots like ChatGPT for news weekly, up from 7% last year, rising to 16% among under-35s. But just 1% called chatbots their main news source. Crucially, only 4% of chatbot users said they 'always' or 'often' clicked through to original sources, compared to 19% from search and 17% from social media — suggesting AI summaries are satisfying information needs without sending traffic to publishers (Nieman Lab).

Trust in news hit an all-time low of 37% globally. People who rely primarily on social media were more negative about coverage of major topics including Ukraine, inflation, and climate change (Reuters Institute).
sections.whyItMatters: The economics of news publishing depend on audiences coming to owned platforms. If chatbots and social feeds are replacing that journey — with click-through rates of just 4% — the business model for original reporting becomes even more precarious at exactly the moment trust in news is at its lowest.

bottomLine: The week's dominant story remains the Anthropic export control order — the first time the US government has weaponized model access as a national security tool, and the mechanism (global shutdown because selective enforcement is infeasible) means every allied government using American AI now knows their access is contingent on Washington's say-so. China moved fast to fill the gap, with Zhipu surging 33% on open-source positioning. The G7's first quantified minerals dependency target and Japan's stockpiling push acknowledge the same pattern in physical supply chains: dependence on a single supplier creates leverage, and reducing it is measured in decades, not years. Meanwhile, the UK's under-16 social media ban and the Reuters Institute data showing social platforms overtaking traditional news both point to the same underlying shift — the infrastructure of information is being rebuilt around platforms whose incentives do not align with accuracy or accountability.
sources: WIRED, Forbes, Fortune, Reason, Anthropic official statement, TNW, CNBC, Bloomberg, CSIS, Reuters, Global Times, Sankei Shimbun, Nikkei Asia, SCMP, Japan Times, Mainichi, UK Government, Oxford Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism, Nieman Lab, Digital Journal, CNN, Kakaku, Adnkronos, Seeking Alpha, Invezz, Quartz
